title: NOC 94202 – Assemblers and inspectors, electrical appliance, apparatus and equipment manufacturing | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s electrical manufacturing sector is a cornerstone of the national "Electrification Strategy." Assemblers and Inspectors (NOC 94202) are the skilled technicians responsible for building and verifying the equipment that powers Canadian homes and industries—ranging from smart kitchen appliances to heavy-duty industrial transformers and power switchgear. As the country moves toward a net-zero grid, international workers with experience in automated assembly and electrical safety testing are seeing a significant surge in demand, particularly in Ontario’s industrial heartland and Quebec’s energy-tech corridor.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Component Assembly: Assembling prefabricated parts and subassemblies into finished electrical appliances or apparatus using hand and power tools.
Wiring & Connection: Reading electrical schematics and blueprints to install and connect internal wiring, coils, and electronic components.
Machine Operation: Operating automated equipment to wind coils or assemble smaller electrical components.
Testing & Inspection: Using digital multimeters, ammeters, and high-voltage test equipment to verify electrical continuity, insulation resistance, and operational safety.
2026 Update: Utilizing digital tablets to log "Smart Diagnostic" data and monitoring automated quality sensors via IoT-connected production dashboards.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Training: On-the-job training is provided; however, a College Certificate in Electrical Techniques or a similar field is highly preferred for 2026 technical roles.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in the same company is often required for advancement to senior assembly or inspection roles.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS and site-specific high-voltage safety training.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market. While automation simplifies some repetitive assembly, the complexity of 2026-grade smart devices requires more specialized Inspectors and Testers. Over 4,500 openings are projected due to the expansion of 5G infrastructure and the modernization of Canada's electrical grid.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by "Steady Growth" as Canada invests in domestic manufacturing to secure its green energy supply chains:
Ontario (GTA & Southwestern Ontario): Very Good prospects. The region is a hub for major appliance and electrical component manufacturers, currently facing a technical labor shortage.
Quebec (Montreal & Montérégie): Strong demand. Driven by Hydro-Québec's infrastructure projects and the expansion of specialized electrical apparatus manufacturing.
Manitoba (Winnipeg): Good prospects. Winnipeg hosts several large-scale manufacturers of commercial kitchen equipment and industrial electrical units.
National Trend: Approximately 15,400 people work in this occupation. The 2026 market favors those who can bridge the gap between mechanical assembly and digital control integration.
Why Work in Canada as an Electrical Assembler?
Green Energy Integration: Work on the frontline of the energy transition, building the smart-grid components and energy-efficient appliances of the future.
High-Tech Environment: Gain experience with 2026-grade technologies, including automated wire-stripping systems, robotic pick-and-place arms, and computerized load-testing stations.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams and is a core qualifier for the 2026 TR to PR transition programs.
Stable, Year-Round Employment: Electrical manufacturing is an essential industry with consistent production cycles and high rates of full-time, permanent positions.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Electrical assemblers are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s manufacturing corridor.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
OINP Express Entry Human Capital Priorities: Periodically targets candidates with experience in specialized manufacturing and electrical sectors.
